1: wayyaʿan ʾîyôḇ — the standard narrative formula (vayya'an + name) that opens each of Job's replies; the book's speeches alternate between three cycles of the friends and Job. Job's reply here comes after Zophar's first speech (ch. 11), where Zophar claimed to know the depths of God and accused Job of hidden sin.
2: ʾattēm ʿām — 'you are the people' — a cutting irony: ʿām normally means 'a people' or 'the people' as a collective, but used singularly of these three men it implies they fancy themselves the entire repository of wisdom. wĕʿimmaḵem tāmûṯ ḥāḵmāh — 'and wisdom will die with you' — sarcasm; if wisdom dies when they die, it is as fragile and contingent as they are. The verse targets the friends' assumed epistemic privilege.
3: lēḇāḇ — 'mind/heart' in the full biblical sense of the seat of understanding and will, not just emotion. nōpēl ʾānōḵî mimmkkem — 'I do not fall below you' — the verb nāpal (to fall) used figuratively for status inferiority; Job denies any intellectual subordination. The rhetorical question 'who does not know such things?' dismantles the friends' claim to special insight: the doctrine they proclaim is the most common possible orthodoxy, not hidden wisdom.
4: śĕḥôq lĕrēʿēhû — 'a laughingstock to his neighbor' — śĕḥôq is laughter in the mocking sense (cf. Gen 21:9, where Ishmael 'mocks'). The following phrase is the scandal: the one who becomes a laughingstock is qārāʾ ʾel-ʾĕlôhîm wayyaʿănēhû — 'who called to God and he answered him.' Answered prayer is supposed to mark the righteous; Job's answered-prayer history makes his current mockery a theological scandal. ṣaddîq tāmîm — 'just and blameless' — the precise epithets from 1:1 and 1:8 (God's own verdict); Job now deploys them of himself, underscoring the contradiction.
5: lappîḏ — torch/lamp; in the thought of the prosperous man, the lamp of the afflicted (one who is stumbling, nākôn lĕmiʿad rāgel = 'ready to slip with the foot') is an object of contempt rather than compassion. The verse indicts the comfort-bias of prosperity theology: the comfortable naturally interpret the stumbling of others as moral failure rather than undeserved calamity.
6: šālēwû — 'undisturbed/at ease' (from šālāh) — the friends' retributive framework is undermined by the observable fact that the tents of violent men are šālēwû while the righteous Job suffers. ʾăšer hēḇîʾ ʾĕlôhîm bĕyādô — 'into whose hand God has brought [it]' — an ironic acknowledgment that God himself facilitates the security of those who provoke him, which is precisely what the friends' theology cannot accommodate.
7: Job shifts to a wisdom-hymn structure using the 'ask the creation' motif — šĕʾal-nāʾ bĕhēmôṯ (ask the animals), ʿôp̄ haššāmayim (birds of the air). The appeal to non-human creation as teacher is a striking rhetorical move: the very creatures who cannot speak or reason are more reliable instructors than the friends. The four creation-witnesses (animals, birds, earth, fish) form an ascending cosmic series.
8: śîaḥ lāʾāreṣ — 'speak to the earth' — śîaḥ normally means to reflect or meditate (the word for meditation in Ps 1:2 is also from this root); here it is the activity of conversing with the earth itself. ḏāgê hayyām yĕsappĕr-lāḵ — 'the fish of the sea will declare it to you' — the fish use sāpar (the verb for counting and narrating); the same verb governs Ps 19:1-2 (the heavens sāpar the glory of God). Creation does theology that the friends have missed.
9: mî lō-yāḏaʿ bĕḵol-ʾēlleh — 'who does not know in all these?' — rhetorical question capping the creation-witness section. The grammatically significant feature is what follows: kî yaḏ-YHWH ʿāśṯāh zōʾṯ — 'that the hand of YHWH has done this.' This is the only occurrence of the divine name YHWH in the poetic core of Job (chs. 3–42:6); throughout the poetry the terms are ʾĒl, Ĕlôah, Šaddai. Most scholars regard v9 as preserving a traditional idiom or cited proverb, since the narrator and the divine speeches freely use YHWH but the poetry avoids it.
10: ʾăšer bĕyāḏô nep̄eš kol-ḥay — 'in whose hand is the life (nepeš) of every living creature' — nepeš here is not 'soul' in the platonic sense but the animating life-force; the full phrase 'the breath of all human flesh' (rûaḥ kol-bĕśar-ʾîš) pairs the immaterial animating breath with its bodily vehicle. Divine sovereignty over all life is the theological premise underlying the creation-teacher argument of vv7-9.
11: hăʾōzen millîn tiḇḥān — 'does not the ear test words?' — the sense-metaphor of tasting: the root of tiḇḥān is bḥn (to test/assay); the parallel ḥēk is the palate/roof of the mouth that discerns taste. The verse rebukes the friends by analogy: as the palate can taste good food from bad, a discerning mind should be able to hear the difference between genuine wisdom and false comfort. The ear-as-taster metaphor recurs in 34:3.
12: bĕyĕšîšîm ḥāḵmāh — 'with the aged is wisdom' — a conventional wisdom maxim the friends would affirm. ûbōrek yāmîm tĕḇûnāh — 'and with length of days, understanding.' Job states the truism ironically, as a setup for v13: these conventional attributes are not the possession of aged humans but of God himself. The verse performs a rhetorical reversal: yes, wisdom belongs to the ancient — the most ancient of all.
13: ʿimmô ḥāḵmāh ûgĕḇûrāh — 'with him are wisdom and strength' — the verse transfers the attributes of v12 (aged human wisdom) to God exclusively; the poetic shift is marked by the pronoun ʿimmô ('with him' = God) displacing the human subject. ʿēṣāh ûṯĕḇûnāh — 'counsel and understanding' — this exact tetrad (ḥāḵmāh, gĕḇûrāh, ʿēṣāh, tĕḇûnāh) reappears in Isa 11:2 as attributes of the messianic king on whom the Spirit rests; Job recites God's essential nature.
14: yihrōs — 'he tears down' (from hāras, to demolish); wĕlō yibbāneh — 'and it cannot be built again' — the absolute finality of divine demolition contrasted with human rebuilding. yisgōr ʿal-ʾîš — 'he shuts up against a man' — the preposition ʿal gives the sense of imprisoning; the parallel is God locking a man in and there being no release (wĕlō yippāṯēaḥ). The verse begins a hymn of divine sovereignty that continues through v25.
15: yiṣrōr — 'he holds back' (from ṣārar, to bind/restrict); applied to waters, the holding-back/releasing pattern demonstrates God's absolute control of natural forces. wĕyišlaḥēm wĕyahpĕḵû ʾāreṣ — 'he releases them and they overturn the earth' — the two antithetical divine acts (withhold/release) frame the absolute range of divine authority over hydrology.
16: ʿōz ûṯûšîyyāh lô — 'strength and effective wisdom are his' — tûšîyyāh is a rare and distinctively Joban term (also in 6:13; 11:6; 26:3; and Proverbs 2:7; 3:21; 8:14), often rendered 'sound wisdom' or 'effective power'; it has the sense of resourcefulness that achieves results. The phrase haššōgēg ûhamašgeh — 'the one who errs and the one who causes to err' (i.e., the deceived and the deceiver) — is striking: both belong to God, which means divine sovereignty comprehends even error and deception, not merely righteousness.
17: môlîḵ yôʿăṣîm šōlāl — 'he leads counselors away stripped' — šōlāl means naked/stripped of possessions, used of prisoners of war being led away stripped; applied to 'counselors' (yôʿăṣîm, plural of yāʿaṣ), it is deeply ironic. wĕšōp̄ĕṭîm yĕhôlēl — 'and turns judges into fools' — hōlēl (from hll used in the pi'el for 'to make foolish') applied to those who administer justice. The reversal-of-dignity hymn begins here and runs through v25.
18: mûsar mĕlāḵîm pittēaḥ — 'he loosens the belt/authority of kings' — mûsar here is not 'discipline' (its common sense) but the bound garment or sash/authority-girdle worn by kings; loosening it symbolizes stripping of royal authority. wayyeʾsōr ʾēzôr bĕmoṯnêhem — 'and girds a servant's cloth around their loins' — the reversal is complete: the royal girdle removed, a servant's cloth applied. The royal humiliation imagery will be echoed in Isa 20 and the suffering-servant passages.
19: hakkōhănîm — priests — Job includes priests in the list of those God strips, alongside counselors, judges, kings (v18), elders (v20), and princes (v21). The comprehensiveness of the list (military, legal, cultic, political leadership) suggests that no human institution is insulated from divine reversal. wĕʾêṯānîm yĕsallēp̄ — 'and overturns those who were established/mighty' — ʾêṯānîm is 'enduring streams' / 'perennial' (cf. Amos 5:24), applied to those who seemed permanently established.
20: sēp̄er sāp̄ at neʾĕmānîm — 'he removes the speech of the trusted' — sāpar in the sense of removing/taking away (cf. Gen 8:13, the surface of the earth 'was dry'); neʾĕmānîm are those who are trustworthy/reliable. ûṭaʿam zĕqēnîm yiqqāḥ — 'and takes away the discernment of the aged' — ṭaʿam is taste/discernment (the same root as v11's palate metaphor), applied here to the deliberative wisdom of elders. The verse directly undermines the friends' appeal to age and reliability as credentials.
21: šôpēḵ bûz ʿal-nĕḏîḇîm — 'he pours contempt on nobles' — the verb šāpak ('to pour out') is used of pouring water or blood; 'contempt/dishonor' poured like water onto those of high status. wĕḥezqaṯ ʾappîqîm rippāh — 'and loosens the strength of the mighty' — ʾappîqîm are 'channels' or 'strong places' (the root means to be strong, to restrain); loosening their strength is the undoing of every earthly power structure.
22: mĕgalleh ʿămûqôṯ miṇ-ḥōšeḵ — 'he brings to light deep things from darkness' — ʿămûqôṯ (deep things, from ʿāmōq) also appears in Dan 2:22 (God 'reveals deep and hidden things') and 1 Cor 2:10 (the Spirit searches 'the depths of God'). ṣalmāwet — 'shadow of death' — one of Job's signature terms (occurring 10 times in Job out of 18 total OT uses); a compound of ṣēl (shadow) + māwet (death), a word for extreme darkness that Ps 23:4 deploys for the valley of mortal danger. God brings even what is hidden in death-shadow into the open.
23: maśgîʾ laggôyim wayyaʾbĕḏēm — 'he makes nations great, then destroys them' — the juxtaposition of national enlargement and destruction within a single verse shows the rapidity and totality of divine sovereignty over history. šōṭēaḥ laggôyim wayyanḥēm — 'he expands them and then leads them away' — nāḥāh can mean to guide/lead (like a shepherd), ironic here since the leading results in exile or dispersal. The verse anticipates the Deuteronomic theology of nations rising and falling by divine decree.
24: mēsîr lēḇ rāʾšê ʿam-hāʾāreṣ — 'he strips the heart from the leaders of the peoples of the earth' — lēḇ (heart = understanding/mind) removed from the ruling class; without lēḇ they cannot govern. wayyatʿēm bĕtōhû lō-ḏāreḵ — 'and makes them wander in tōhû without a path' — tōhû is the primordial formlessness of Gen 1:2 ('without form and void'); the cosmos-undoing word applied to the condition of leaderless nations stumbling through pathless wilderness. The cosmic resonance is deliberate.
25: yĕmašaššû-ḥōšeḵ wĕlō-ʾôr — 'they grope in darkness without light' — māšaš is tactile groping, the same verb used of the blind Philistines in Judg 16:26 and of the Egyptians in the darkness plague (Exod 10:21). wayyatʿēm kĕšikkôr — 'and he makes them stagger like a drunkard' — the drunkenness motif for divine disorientation recurs in Isa 29:9; 63:6; Jer 25:27. The chapter ends with the powerful image of once-glorious national leaders reduced to blind, lurching figures in the dark.
